column2: — I know.
— Then why are you here?
— Because I didn't promise you any such thing.
— Where do you want to go?
— I don't care.
— That's not true.
— I want to go to the big water.
— And when can you?
— Right now, even.
— Well, let's go.
— I'll settle the bill.
Miyechka got up and went to the bar counter.

Her mood suddenly improved, she decided for herself that she wouldn't think about anything, that here were a few days and they could be lived, and altogether she had promised herself that this summer would be outside time, outside space, outside everything.
And besides, she had a little shelter, a refuge to which she could return, where, by happy and unhappy circumstances, only women lived, except for the grandmother's ginger cat.
They set off that same evening.
The sun blinded their eyes, Miyechka squinted, curled up on the front seat, and told stories about when she was little.
Trucks stood along the highway, striped watermelons spilling out of them.
They stopped.
— You go out, — said Ustym.
Miyechka got out in her strappy tank top.
The watermelon seller stood in profile.
His belly was the size of three medium watermelons.
— Good evening, — said Miyechka.
He looked at her against the sun.
— How many?
— he finally spoke.
— One, — retorted Miyechka.
— Choose whichever one you want.
Miyechka stood and looked at the watermelons.
They were all equally striped, equally gleaming in the sun, equally green.
She remembered about the dry little stem.
There were no little stems.
She felt helplessness and powerlessness rolling over her.
Then suddenly a tenderness rolled over her toward the pile of watermelons, toward each of them.
She understood that it didn't matter which one she took, it changed nothing.
— Are you going to take one?
— asked the seller.
In the middle of his belly, a little asymmetrically, slanting down and to the left, his navel showed.
Miyechka nodded.
She put the watermelon on her legs and rode with it in her lap, then put it on the floor mat and rested her bare heels on it.
The watermelon was smooth and warm.
— You know, — she said to Ustym, — my grandmother sees the dead in photographs.
— How's that?
— Ustym watched the road, in his glasses flickered the oncoming cars that occasionally drove along the evening highway.
— She takes a photo and sees those who were still alive back then.
Even though they're not in the photo.
Miyechka took out her phone and found an old black-and-white photograph of a woman sitting at a table.
On the table stood a bowl of pears.
The woman looks straight ahead with a barely noticeable smile, on her face there isn't a shadow of a pose.
— This is my great-grandmother, — said Miyechka.
Ustym cast a quick glance at the photo.
— Grandmother, when she looks at this photo, sees that it's August, because of the pears, — Miyechka went on.
— And great-grandfather passed away in November.
And she says — so great-grandfather was still alive then.
And his sister was still alive then too, she outlived him by two years.
And Aunt Toma had only just died.
You see?
She sees those who aren't in the photo.
— It's like me, in every historical photo I see whoever was General Secretary at the time.
— How's that?
— Well, some aunt recently shows her wedding photo, and I think — so she got married back under Andropov.
They arrived when it was dark.
On the seashore music thundered and occasional shouts rang out.
Along the water people walked selling cold beer.
It smelled of corn, alcohol and some kind of meat.
— Well, dear, are you glad?
— asked Ustym and smiled.
— I dreamed of it all my life, — answered Miyechka.
— Hungry?
Shall I buy you a little kebab?
— Ha-ha.
Not far from the sea they saw several hotels.
Two had no vacancies.
In one only the 'deluxe' was left.
The hotel was called 'Hortensia.'
'Deluxe' meant that the toilet was in the room.
They were met by the landlady in a wide blue robe with little pink flowers.
Her arms were dark brown, while under her armpits white skin flashed now and then.
It was hard to believe that she was like this in winter.
Or maybe, as is often the way by the sea, she herself had never even been to the beach.
— I'm Lida, — said the landlady.
— Good evening, — said Ustym.
Miyechka was holding the watermelon.
She had no other things.
She wanted to sleep and to eat.
— Why are you so helpless, sweetheart?
— Ustym put his arm around her shoulders.
— Or aren't you glad of the sea?
— Everything's okay, — said Miyechka and pressed the watermelon to herself.
She felt very sorry for herself.
She was not a little bird, as Danylo called her, but a little parrot that had treacherously escaped its cage and found itself in a cruel world.
Lida took a bunch of keys, detached the one with a plaster stuck on it bearing the worn inscription '8,' and gave it to Ustym.
— Take it for at least seven days, why don't you, — said Lida.
— Later you'll decide you want it, and there'll be no rooms left.
— Thank you, we'd just like to spend the night, — retorted Miyechka.
In the room it was hot and there was a stifling smell.
— At last we're at a resort, — Ustym joked.
— Just as you dreamed.
— Hurray!
— Miyechka cried out with feigned enthusiasm.
— It's so hot and I so want to hold you, — said Ustym.
Tears welled up in Miyechka's eyes.
She realized that over the whole day, apart from a smile, he hadn't said a single kind word to her.
And she remembered all those wise words that had been so easy for her to follow.
'Don't be a burden,' everyone said. 'A woman should be spontaneous, light and joyful.'
Very easy to do with everyone, except him.
Ustym came up to Miyechka, finally took the watermelon out of her hands.
And said:
— I love you, my little bird.
Miyechka wanted to embrace him, but there was a watermelon between them.
They put it on one of the beds.
The deluxe room had two single beds with pillows folded into triangles.
And an hour later you could still hear the drunken shouts and the thumping of bass from the shore.
Behind the wall a child was crying.
In the courtyard of the 'Hortensia' hotel a dog howled.
Lida, perhaps, was already asleep.
In room eight of the 'Hortensia' hotel it was hot, but cozy in the way it can only be for two dear people alone with each other.
Miyechka came out of the shower, where along with herself she'd washed all her clothes, since she had no others with her.
Through the window coolness suddenly burst in, and it smelled of rain.
Down in the courtyard grapevines grew, where Lida hid from the southern sun during the day.
The grape leaves began to flutter.
Behind the wall the child began crying again.
The rain began.
The child was crying.
Ustym embraced Miyechka, still wet from the shower.
There was a knock at the door.
— Did you come by car?
— in the corridor stood Lida in a white nightgown.
— Yes, — answered Ustym.
— In the next room the little one's in a bad way, and there's no doctor here, it's twenty kilometers away, — Lida rattled on, gasping for air.
Quick footsteps were heard in the corridor, a woman was running with a child in her arms.
Ustym put on his shorts, Miyechka put on her just-washed wet tank top.
They grabbed their things, threw them into the car.
The woman got into the back seat.
The child was uncontrollably nauseous.
You couldn't tell whether it was a girl or a boy, but the woman whispered to it 'Irochka.'
Irochka was rolling her eyes and wanted to pass out.
Ustym poured water into her, twisted around from the front seat.
The water came right back up.
The car was shaken on the road.
At night Miyechka could see very badly and was afraid of losing a wheel in a pothole.
They drove into the district center.
There was almost no lighting.
Along both sides of the road grew southern poplars with white trunks.
Ustym took the child in his arms and walked at a brisk pace into the district hospital.
The doctors came running.
The woman whispered some words of thanks and cried.
It was four in the morning.
They drove out of the district center, over which the sky was only just beginning to lighten, turning from black to dark gray.
The highway ran along the sea.
The taverns stood as if dead, Miyechka was cold from her wet clothes.
Tents stood on the sand.
They drove farther from the taverns and the tents and stopped on the beach.
The sea was calm, the way it is after rain.
Dove-gray in color with a light steam over the water, it was like a cloud.
Ustym got out his own shirt for Miyechka, she undressed while he tested the water with his foot.
— The water is warmer than the air, — she said, coming up behind him and touching the water with her fingers.
— If you remembered even a little physics, — retorted Ustym, — you could have predicted that.
The sea is steaming, can't you see?
Miyechka embraced him and said:
— Let's go home.
On the way they ate the watermelon.
Miyechka dug out the flesh with a pocket knife and handed pieces to Ustym.
She splattered her legs, the seat, Ustym.
Then they swam in a quarry and cleaned the car of the night's adventures with the child.
Lida called and said they'd forgotten their flip-flops in the room.
Miyechka said she was fine barefoot too.
She lay on the water, and beneath her — hundreds of meters of quarry.
She never felt at ease in water, and when she thought about those hundreds of meters beneath her, it became frightening.
So she kept glancing at the shore, barely raising her head from the water, and the figure of Ustym calmed her.
He pretended he wasn't looking at her, but stood there and kept glancing the whole time.
Water sat in Miyechka's ears.
There was a roaring.
From the quarry it was still a few hours' drive.
— Do you feel how good it is here?
— asked Miyechka.
— Where?
— Right here, in the car.

— Of course, we washed off all the vomit, so right away it's good, — said Ustym, and then he smiled at her so warmly and so sadly that Miyechka had to turn away to the window, so as not to embitter this goodness spilled in the air with her self-pity.
No happiness with him, not a single moment when it seemed that this was what made life worth living, could be lived all the way to the end.
Over this relationship, which had come from nowhere, some sort of finiteness always loomed.
Ustym drove Miyechka to the apple orchard.
She got out of the car barefoot, laughing.
For the last hour he had been telling her various student tales.
They were always about large doses of alcohol and friendship, which, in the storyteller's own conviction, can exist only between men.
Together they came up to the apple trees.
Ustym repeated that it had to be uprooted.
Ahead lay a hot day.

Miyechka thought about why he was leaving, why she was staying here, why everything had ended so quickly, but she knew that if she said even one of these phrases aloud, she would burst into tears in an instant.
So aloud she said only: 'Have a good day.'
